title: Decimals
blurb: Place value past the dot, comparing, rounding, and the four operations — built on a 10×10 grid you can shade.

lessons.title: Decimal Place Value
lessons.concreteComparison: A price tag of \$2.45 means 2 whole dollars, 4 dimes, and 5 pennies. A dime is one **tenth** of a dollar and a penny is one **hundredth** — so the digits after the dot are counting tenths and hundredths. That dot is a **decimal point**, and $2.45$ is a **decimal**.
lessons.objective: Read and write decimals to the thousandths place, name the value of each digit, and see a decimal as a fraction with a denominator of 10, 100, or 1000.
concept: A **decimal** uses a dot to show parts of a whole smaller than 1. The first digit after the dot is **tenths** (out of 10), the next is **hundredths** (out of 100), the next is **thousandths** (out of 1000).
Each place is **10 times smaller** than the one to its left — the same pattern as ones, tens, hundreds, just running the other way past the dot.
Because the places are tenths, hundredths, and thousandths, every decimal is just a **fraction with a denominator that is a power of 10**. So $0.37 = \frac{37}{100}$.
lessons.keyIdea: The digits after the decimal point are tenths, hundredths, thousandths — each place 10× smaller than the last. A decimal is a fraction whose denominator is 10, 100, or 1000.

facts.title: Place value after the dot
facts.explanation: Tenths, then hundredths, then thousandths — each ten times smaller.

workedExamples.solution: The last digit (7) is in the hundredths place, so the denominator is 100: $0.37 = \frac{37}{100}$.

workedExamples.statement: Write $0.6$ as a fraction in simplest form.
workedExamples.solution: One digit after the dot means tenths: $0.6 = \frac{6}{10} = \frac{3}{5}$.

commonMistakes.text: Reading $0.6$ and $0.06$ as the same size.
commonMistakes.correction: $0.6$ is six tenths; $0.06$ is six hundredths — ten times smaller.
Count the digits after the dot: two digits → hundredths → $\frac{37}{100}$.

tryIt.statement: Write $0.08$ as a fraction in simplest form.
tryIt.solution: The 8 is in the hundredths place: $0.08 = \frac{8}{100} = \frac{2}{25}$.

tryIt.statement: Write $0.125$ as a fraction in simplest form.
tryIt.solution: Three digits after the dot means thousandths: $0.125 = \frac{125}{1000} = \frac{1}{8}$.

steps.title: One whole, cut into 100
steps.teach: The big square is **one whole**. Each small square is one **hundredth** ($0.01$), and a full column of ten is one **tenth** ($0.1$). Tap to build a decimal and watch the fraction keep up.

steps.title: Tenths and hundredths
beats: The first digit after the dot counts **tenths** — full columns of the grid.
The second digit counts **hundredths** — single squares.
So $0.37$ is **3 tenths and 7 hundredths**: three full columns plus seven squares.
Together that is $\frac{3}{10} + \frac{7}{100} = \frac{37}{100}$.

steps.title: A decimal is a fraction in disguise
beats: Count the digits after the dot — that tells you the denominator.
One digit → tenths (over 10). Two digits → hundredths (over 100).
$0.6$ is six full columns: $\frac{6}{10}$, which simplifies to $\frac{3}{5}$.
Same amount, two costumes — a decimal and a fraction.

steps.title: Reading a decimal as a fraction
steps.intro: Count the digits after the dot to choose the denominator, then simplify if you can.

steps: The last digit (7) is in the **hundredths** place, so the denominator is 100.
$0.37 = \frac{37}{100}$ — nothing divides 37 and 100, so it is already simplest.

examples.prompt: Write $0.6$ as a fraction in simplest form.
steps: One digit after the dot → **tenths**, so $0.6 = \frac{6}{10}$.

steps.title: Read the grid
steps.intro: Each grid is one whole. Count the columns (tenths) and squares (hundredths).

problems.prompt: Which decimal does this grid show?

problems.explanation: The 8 is in the hundredths place → $\frac{8}{100}$.

problems.prompt: What is $0.05$ as a fraction in simplest form?

steps.title: One more place: thousandths
beats: Add a third digit after the dot and you reach **thousandths** — out of 1000.
$0.125$ means 1 tenth, 2 hundredths, and 5 thousandths.
As a fraction that is $\frac{125}{1000}$, which simplifies to $\frac{1}{8}$.
Every step right past the dot makes the place ten times smaller.

steps.title: Naming the place value
steps.intro: The place tells you the value: 1st = tenths, 2nd = hundredths, 3rd = thousandths.

examples.prompt: In $0.125$, what is the value of the digit **2**?
steps: The 2 is the **second** digit after the dot → hundredths.

examples.prompt: Write $0.125$ as a fraction in simplest form.
steps: Three digits after the dot → **thousandths**, so $\frac{125}{1000}$.

steps.title: Which place, which value?
steps.intro: Read the position of each digit after the dot.

problems.prompt: In $0.349$, which digit is in the **thousandths** place?
problems.explanation: Third place after the dot = thousandths = 9.

problems.prompt: Which is **larger**: the value of 4 in $0.4$, or the value of 4 in $0.04$?
Write $0.2$ as a fraction in simplest form.

steps.title: You already read decimals every day
steps.body: Every price tag is a decimal. \$4.99 is 4 whole dollars and 99 hundredths of a dollar — 99 cents. You have been reading hundredths your whole life.

steps.title: Decimal to fraction
steps.explanation: Two digits after the dot → hundredths → $\frac{37}{100}$.

steps.title: What you learned
points: The places after the dot are **tenths, hundredths, thousandths** — each 10× smaller than the last.
A decimal is a **fraction over 10, 100, or 1000**: $0.37 = \frac{37}{100}$.
Count the digits after the dot to pick the denominator, then simplify.

lessons.title: Comparing & Ordering Decimals
lessons.concreteComparison: A pen costs \$0.50 and a pencil \$0.45. Which is cheaper? The pencil: $0.45 < 0.50$ — even though 45 looks bigger than 5, because $0.45$ is four-tenths-and-a-bit while $0.50$ is five tenths. Comparing decimals means comparing **place by place**, not counting digits.
lessons.objective: Compare two decimals by lining up the decimal points and checking place value from the left, and put several decimals in order — knowing that extra digits do not make a decimal larger.
concept: To compare decimals, **line up the decimal points** and read from the **left**: tenths first, then hundredths, then thousandths.
The first place where the digits differ decides it. For $0.5$ and $0.45$, the tenths are 5 vs 4, so $0.5 > 0.45$.
Putting a zero on the **end** changes nothing: $0.5 = 0.50$. Writing both with the same number of places makes them easy to line up.
lessons.keyIdea: Compare from the left, one place at a time — the first place that differs wins. More digits does NOT mean a bigger decimal.

facts.title: Trailing zeros are free
facts.explanation: Zeros on the right don't change the value; they just show more places.

commonMistakes.correction: Line up places: tenths 4 vs 5, so $0.45 < 0.5$. Digit count doesn't decide size.
$0.5 = 0.50$ — a trailing zero adds no value.

steps.title: Which one is bigger?
steps.teach: Both grids are one whole. $0.5$ fills five full columns; $0.45$ fills four columns and five squares. Tap the sign that makes it true — the shaded amount never lies.

steps.title: Compare from the left
beats: Line up the decimal points and read **left to right**.
The first place that differs decides it — here the **tenths**: 5 beats 4.
So $0.5 > 0.45$, even though 45 has more digits than 5.
On a number line, $0.45$ sits just to the left of $0.5$.

steps.title: Trailing zeros are free
beats: Adding a zero on the **end** of a decimal doesn't change its value: $0.5 = 0.50$.
That lets you give two decimals the **same number of places** before comparing.
To compare $0.5$ and $0.45$, write $0.50$ vs $0.45$ — now just compare $50$ and $45$ hundredths.

steps.title: Comparing two decimals
steps.intro: Line up the points, then compare from the left until the digits differ.

steps.title: Which is larger?
steps.intro: Compare from the left. A number line can help you picture it.

steps.title: Putting decimals in order
beats: To order several decimals, compare the **tenths** first and group them.
Break ties by the **hundredths**, then the **thousandths**.
$0.25$, $0.3$, $0.4$ → tenths 2, 3, 4 → already in order.
Plot them and the order is the left-to-right order on the line.

steps.title: Ordering decimals
steps.intro: Compare tenths first; break ties with the next place.

steps: $0.06$ has 0 tenths; $0.6$ and $0.66$ both have 6 tenths.
Break the tie by hundredths: 0 vs 6, so $0.6 < 0.66$.

steps.title: Order them
steps.intro: Tenths first, then break ties.

problems.prompt: Which ordering (least to greatest) is correct?

steps.title: Hundredths win medals
steps.body: At the 2008 Olympics, swimmer Michael Phelps won a gold medal by **one hundredth of a second** — $0.01$ s. Comparing decimals out to the hundredths place was the whole difference between gold and silver.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Line up the decimal points and compare **from the left**, place by place.
The first place that differs decides it — more digits doesn't mean bigger.
Trailing zeros don't change a decimal: $0.5 = 0.50$.

lessons.title: Rounding Decimals
lessons.concreteComparison: A jacket is priced at \$39.95. Rounded to the nearest dollar that's \$40 — close enough to budget with. **Rounding** swaps an exact decimal for a simpler nearby one: you give up a little precision to get an easier number.
lessons.objective: Round a decimal to the nearest whole number, tenth, or hundredth by finding the nearest mark on the number line, using the rule that the next digit being 5 or more rounds up.
concept: To **round**, pick the place you want — whole, tenth, or hundredth — and find the **nearest** value at that place.
Look at the digit **just after** that place. If it is **5 or more**, round up; if it is **4 or less**, keep the digit (round down).
On a number line, rounding is choosing the closer mark. A digit of 5 lands exactly halfway, and by rule that **rounds up**.
lessons.keyIdea: Look at the digit right after your rounding place: 5 or more rounds up, 4 or less rounds down. It is the nearest mark on the number line.

facts.title: The rounding rule
facts.explanation: Check the single digit just after the place you're rounding to.

workedExamples.solution: $0.37$ is between $0.3$ and $0.4$; the next digit is 7 (≥5), so round up to $0.4$.

workedExamples.statement: Round $3.7$ to the nearest whole number.
workedExamples.solution: $3.7$ is between $3$ and $4$; the next digit is 7 (≥5), so round up to $4$.

commonMistakes.text: Rounding $0.62$ up to $0.7$ because there are digits after the tenths.
commonMistakes.correction: Only the **next single digit** matters: $0.62$ → next digit 2 (≤4) → stays $0.6$.

commonMistakes.text: Forgetting that a digit of 5 rounds up.
commonMistakes.correction: Exactly 5 is the halfway point; the rule sends it up.

tryIt.solution: Next digit is 3 (≤4), so round down to $0.8$.

tryIt.statement: Round $2.4$ to the nearest whole number.
tryIt.solution: Next digit is 4 (≤4), so round down to $2$.

steps.title: Which mark is it closer to?
steps.teach: $0.37$ sits between $0.3$ and $0.4$. It is past the halfway mark $0.35$, so it is closer to $0.4$. Tap the mark it rounds to.

steps.title: Rounding is the nearest mark
beats: Rounding to the nearest tenth asks: which tenth-mark is closest?
$0.37$ is between $0.3$ and $0.4$.
It is past the halfway point $0.35$, so it rounds to $0.4$.
The number line shows it sitting nearer the $0.4$ mark.

steps.title: The 5-or-more rule
beats: You don't always need a picture — just look at the **next digit**.
Rounding to tenths? Look at the **hundredths** digit.
$5$ or more → round the tenth **up**; $4$ or less → leave it.
A digit of exactly 5 is halfway, and the rule sends it **up**: $0.35 \to 0.4$.

steps.title: Rounding to the nearest tenth
steps.intro: Find the two tenths it sits between, then check the hundredths digit.

steps: Next digit is 2 ≤ 4 → round down (keep it).

steps.title: Round to the nearest tenth
steps.intro: Check the hundredths digit: 5 or more rounds up.

problems.explanation: $0.55$ is exactly halfway between $0.5$ and $0.6$; the rule rounds it up to $0.6$.

steps.title: Whole numbers and hundredths
beats: The same rule works at every place — just look one digit to the right.
Nearest **whole**: check the tenths digit. $3.7 \to 4$ (7 ≥ 5).
Nearest **hundredth**: check the thousandths digit. $0.348 \to 0.35$ (8 ≥ 5).
Always check the one digit just past the place you want.

steps.title: Rounding to other places
steps.intro: Whole → check tenths. Hundredth → check thousandths.

examples.prompt: Round $3.7$ to the nearest whole number.

steps.title: Round it
steps.intro: Pick the place, then check the one digit to its right.

problems.prompt: Round $1.49$ to the nearest whole number.
problems.explanation: Tenths digit is 4 ≤ 4 → round down to $1$ (don't let the 9 fool you).
$0.275$ is exactly halfway between $0.27$ and $0.28$; the rule rounds up to $0.28$.

problems.prompt: Round $5.82$ to the nearest whole number.
problems.explanation: Tenths digit is 8 ≥ 5 → round up to $6$.

steps.title: Stores round to the penny
steps.body: Sales tax often makes a price like \$4.937. Stores round to the nearest **hundredth** — the penny — so you actually pay \$4.94. Rounding to two places is everywhere money is.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Rounding swaps a decimal for the **nearest** value at the place you want.
Check the **one digit** just after that place: 5 or more rounds up, 4 or less rounds down.
On a number line, you're just picking the closer mark — and halfway rounds up.

lessons.title: Adding & Subtracting Decimals
lessons.concreteComparison: You buy a drink for \$1.50 and a snack for \$2.35. The total is \$3.85 — you line up dollars with dollars and cents with cents, then add. Paying with \$5.00, your change is $5.00 - 3.85 = \$1.15$. Lining up the decimal point is the whole trick.
lessons.objective: Add and subtract decimals by lining up the decimal points, filling missing places with zeros, then adding or subtracting column by column and bringing the point straight down.
concept: To add or subtract decimals, **line up the decimal points** so tenths sit under tenths and hundredths under hundredths.
Fill any missing places with **zeros** so both numbers have the same number of decimal places.
Then add or subtract column by column just like whole numbers — carrying or borrowing — and bring the decimal point **straight down** into the answer.
lessons.keyIdea: Line up the decimal points, pad with zeros so the places match, then add or subtract column by column — and drop the decimal point straight down.

facts.title: Pad with zeros to line up
facts.explanation: A trailing zero makes the places match so every column lines up.

workedExamples.solution: Line up the points and pad: $2.40 + 1.35 = 3.75$.

commonMistakes.text: Right-aligning the digits instead of the decimal points (adding $2.4 + 1.35$ as if $2.4$ were $2.04$).
commonMistakes.correction: Line up the **decimal points**, not the right edges. Pad: $2.40 + 1.35$.

commonMistakes.text: Forgetting to bring the decimal point straight down into the answer.
commonMistakes.correction: The answer's decimal point sits directly below the others.

steps.title: Line up and add
steps.teach: Stack the numbers with the decimal points in a vertical line. $2.4$ becomes $2.40$ so the places match — the faded zero is a placeholder. Tap to add column by column.

steps.title: Line up the points
beats: Write the numbers stacked, **decimal points in a vertical line**.
Pad with zeros so both have the same places: $2.4 \to 2.40$.
Add column by column from the right, carrying like normal.
Bring the decimal point **straight down**: $2.40 + 1.35 = 3.75$.

steps.title: Why line up, not right-align
beats: Lining up the points keeps **like places together**: tenths add to tenths.
If you right-aligned $2.4$ and $1.35$, the 4 tenths would land under the 5 hundredths — wrong.
Padding $2.4$ to $2.40$ fixes it so every column matches.
$0.3 + 0.45 = 0.75$: three tenths plus four tenths and five hundredths.

steps.title: Adding decimals
steps.intro: Pad to equal places, add each column, drop the point down.

steps: Pad $2.4 \to 2.40$ and line up the points.

steps.title: Add them
steps.intro: Line up the points and pad with zeros first.

steps.title: Subtracting works the same way
beats: Stack with the points lined up and pad: $5 \to 5.00$.
Subtract column by column from the right, **borrowing** when you need to.
Bring the decimal point straight down.
$5.00 - 3.85 = 1.15$ — your change from a \$5 bill.

steps.title: Subtracting decimals
steps.intro: Pad to equal places, then subtract with borrowing.

steps: Pad $5 \to 5.00$ and line up the points.
Both already have one decimal place.

steps.title: Subtract them
steps.intro: Pad to equal places, then borrow as needed.

steps.title: Every cash register does this
steps.body: When a cashier hands you change, they're subtracting decimals: amount paid minus total. A \$20 bill for a \$13.46 order gives $20.00 - 13.46 = \$6.54$ back — lined up and subtracted, just like you're learning.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Line up the **decimal points** — never the right edges.
Pad with zeros so both numbers have the same number of places.
Add or subtract column by column and bring the point **straight down**.

lessons.title: Multiplying Decimals
lessons.concreteComparison: A tile is $0.4$ m wide and $0.3$ m tall. Its area is length × width $= 0.4 \times 0.3 = 0.12$ square meters — about an eighth of a square meter. Multiplying decimals finds areas, scales recipes, and totals prices.
lessons.objective: Multiply decimals by multiplying as if they were whole numbers, then placing the decimal point so the answer has as many places as the two factors combined.
concept: To multiply decimals, **ignore the points** at first and multiply the digits like whole numbers.
Then **count the decimal places** in both factors and give the answer that many places.
$0.3 \times 0.4$: multiply $3 \times 4 = 12$; the factors have $1 + 1 = 2$ places, so the answer is $0.12$. Multiplying by a number **less than 1** makes the result smaller.
lessons.keyIdea: Multiply as whole numbers, then count the total decimal places in both factors — the answer gets that many places.

facts.title: Count the places
facts.explanation: Multiply 3×4 = 12, then place the point 2 digits from the right.

commonMistakes.text: Lining up the decimal points like you do for addition.
commonMistakes.correction: For multiplication you don't line up points — multiply the digits, then **count places** to place the point.

steps.title: The area is the product
steps.teach: Let one factor run **across** and the other run **up** the unit square. The overlap rectangle is the product: $0.3 \times 0.4$ fills a $3 \times 4 = 12$-square corner, which is $0.12$. Change the factors and watch the area.

steps.title: Multiply, then count the places
beats: First ignore the points and multiply: $3 \times 4 = 12$.
Then count the decimal places in the factors: $0.3$ has 1, $0.4$ has 1, so $1+1=2$.
Give the answer 2 places: $0.12$.
It matches the grid — 12 of the 100 little squares are shaded.

steps.title: Multiplying can make things smaller
beats: When you multiply by a number **less than 1**, the result shrinks.
$0.6 \times 0.5 = 0.30$ — smaller than both $0.6$ and $0.5$.
On the grid, the product rectangle is only part of each strip.
That's why half of something ($\times 0.5$) is less than the whole.

steps.title: Multiplying decimals
steps.intro: Multiply the digits, then count the places in both factors.

steps.title: Multiply them
steps.intro: Multiply the digits, then count both factors' places.

steps.title: Counting places, every time
beats: The rule never changes — multiply, then count places.
A trailing zero can be dropped once the point is placed.

steps.title: More products
steps.intro: Count carefully when the factors have more digits.

steps.title: More multiplying
steps.intro: Multiply the digits, then place the point by counting places.

steps.title: Times 0.1 just shifts the point
steps.body: Multiplying by $0.1$ moves the decimal point one place left: $7 \times 0.1 = 0.7$, and $0.7 \times 0.1 = 0.07$. Each $0.1$ makes a number ten times smaller — the mirror image of multiplying by 10.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Multiply the digits as if there were no decimal points.
Count the decimal places in **both** factors; the answer gets that many.
Multiplying by a number less than 1 makes the result smaller.

lessons.title: Dividing Decimals
lessons.concreteComparison: A \$4.80 pizza split among 4 friends is $4.80 \div 4 = \$1.20$ each. And if ribbon costs \$0.60 a meter, \$4.80 buys $4.80 \div 0.6 = 8$ meters. Dividing decimals shares amounts and finds how many fit.
lessons.objective: Divide a decimal by a whole number by bringing the point straight up, and divide by a decimal by shifting both numbers to make the divisor a whole number first.
concept: Dividing a decimal by a **whole number** works like normal division — just bring the decimal point **straight up** into the answer: $4.8 \div 4 = 1.2$.
To divide **by a decimal**, first make the divisor a whole number: multiply **both** numbers by 10, 100, … the same amount.
Scaling both by the same factor doesn't change the answer — it's an equivalent fraction: $4.8 \div 0.6 = 48 \div 6 = 8$.
lessons.keyIdea: Divide by a whole number → bring the point straight up. Divide by a decimal → shift both numbers to make the divisor whole, then divide. Scaling both keeps the answer the same.

facts.title: Make the divisor whole
facts.explanation: Multiply both by 10 — the quotient is unchanged.

commonMistakes.text: Only shifting the divisor's decimal point and forgetting the dividend.
commonMistakes.correction: Shift **both** numbers the same amount, or the answer changes.

commonMistakes.text: Losing the decimal point when dividing by a whole number.
commonMistakes.correction: Bring the point straight up — it sits directly above the point in the dividend.

steps.title: Make the divisor whole
steps.teach: You can't easily divide by $0.6$. Multiply **both** numbers by 10 so the divisor becomes a whole number — the answer doesn't change. Then $48 \div 6 = 8$.

steps.title: Dividing by a whole number
beats: Dividing by a **whole number** is ordinary long division.
Divide the digits as usual and bring the decimal point **straight up**.
The point in the answer sits right above the point in $4.8$.

steps.title: Dividing by a decimal
beats: You can't easily divide by $0.6$ — so make the divisor a **whole number**.
Multiply **both** numbers by 10: $4.8 \div 0.6$ becomes $48 \div 6$.
Scaling both by the same amount keeps the answer the same.

steps.title: Dividing by a whole number
steps.intro: Divide normally; bring the decimal point straight up.

steps.title: Divide by a whole number
steps.intro: Bring the decimal point straight up into the answer.

steps.title: Shifting both keeps the answer
beats: A division is a fraction: $4.8 \div 0.6 = \frac{4.8}{0.6}$.
Multiplying top and bottom by the same number gives an **equivalent fraction**.
$\frac{4.8}{0.6} = \frac{48}{6}$ — same value, easier numbers.
That's exactly the equivalent-fractions move from earlier, used for division.

steps.title: Dividing by a decimal
steps.intro: Shift both numbers until the divisor is whole, then divide.

steps: Divisor has 1 place: multiply both by 10 → $48 \div 6$.
Divisor has 2 places: multiply both by 100 → $75 \div 25$.

steps.title: Divide by a decimal
steps.intro: Make the divisor whole by shifting both numbers.

steps.title: Dividing finds the unit price
steps.body: A 0.5 kg block of cheese costs \$4.00? Divide to find the price per kg: $4.00 \div 0.5 = \$8$ per kg. Shops compare deals by dividing price by amount — decimal division in the real world.

steps.title: What you learned
points: By a **whole number**: divide normally and bring the point straight up.
By a **decimal**: multiply both numbers by 10, 100, … to make the divisor whole.
Shifting both the same amount keeps the answer the same.
